sections.sourcing: No new sourcing-country shifts surfaced this week. F&F's supply base remains anchored across China, India, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka and Turkey via the Hong Kong-headquartered Tesco Sourcing team; Tier 1 factory disclosure lists remain the reference point for supplier vetting. Separately, Tesco continues expanding F&F's franchise footprint into the Middle East (with Al Futtaim in Dubai) and Central Asia (Kazakhstan, Georgia, Armenia, Azerbaijan via Al Hokair), which over time could add volume pressure on existing vendors as store count grows.
sections.strategy: No fresh strategy announcement this week; ongoing context still dominates. F&F is mid-repositioning as a 'design-led' high-street competitor rather than a grocery add-on: tighter trend-led edits, fewer high-volume repeat styles, and more emphasis on longevity/responsible sourcing, per recent trade coverage. The May 2026 online relaunch (2,200+ SKUs via tesco.com and the Clubcard app) continues to underpin digital growth, with further range expansion planned 'over coming months' under clothing & home MD Katja Ahola Klamkin (ex-H&M).
sections.launches: No brand-new collection this week. The BBH London-created 'Style It Out' campaign (launched mid-May, running through summer) continues to run, spotlighting the versatility of F&F's summer range through comic travel-chaos vignettes — a continuation of last year's 'Style Your Way Out' platform rather than a new one.
soWhat: Sourcing footprint is stable — no reallocation signals this week — but franchise expansion into the Middle East and Central Asia is a slow-building demand driver worth tracking for future order-volume upside.

F&F's continued push toward 'design-led' positioning and fewer high-volume repeat styles could mean smaller, more frequent orders and higher design/quality expectations from vendors rather than sheer volume growth.

With FY25/26 results (sales +5.1% to £1.2bn) and the online relaunch now several months old, expect the next hard data point (interim results) to be the trigger for any real vendor-facing news — this week is a holding pattern.
